messages.content:
HOW YOUR CLAIM BECOMES A NUMBER (the perturbation contract):
- You never state € impacts. You emit machine-readable ops; the engine computes
  ΔTAM/ΔSAM/ΔYAM by re-running the real funnel. Any € figure you write in prose
  will be stripped.
- Ops: {nodeId, op: "set"|"scale"|"add", value} rewrite one leaf's value;
  {nodeId, op: "exclude"} removes a dimension cell from the venture's reachable
  scope ("unavailable to us" — foreclosure), which is different from shrinking it.
- Ops apply in array order. Ratio nodes must stay inside [0,1]; tamBase must stay > 0.
- Only FUNNEL-ACTIVE nodes may be perturbed (marked in the ledger you receive).
  shape.cagr and shape.cr3 are context — a demand-pace or concentration risk must
  be translated into the funnel nodes it would actually move (tamBase level,
  serviceableFactor reach, obtainableFactor pace, or a share).
- Sibling shares are NOT re-normalized when you move one share (documented v1
  simplification). If you mean a mix-SHIFT, perturb multiple siblings; if you
  mean "this slice is smaller in absolute terms", one op is correct.
- Perturbing an assumption leaf overrides whatever the user set: your claim is
  about the world, not about their slider.

Fix the hypothesis's targetNodes/perturbation so it passes validation WITHOUT changing the claim's meaning. If the claim cannot be expressed against funnel-active nodes, translate it into the funnel nodes it would actually move.

FUNNEL-ACTIVE NODES:
tamBase (EUR_M, value 300)
geo.DE (ratio, value 0.5)
geo.NL (ratio, value 0.28)
geo.PL (ratio, value 0.07)
geo.CH (ratio, value 0.08)
geo.CZ (ratio, value 0.02)
geo.AT (ratio, value 0.02)
geo.other (ratio, value 0.03)
seg.hyperscale (ratio, value 0.44)
seg.colocation (ratio, value 0.18)
seg.enterprise (ratio, value 0.29)
seg.edge (ratio, value 0.05)
seg.telecom (ratio, value 0.04)
cust.operator-large (ratio, value 0.4)
cust.operator-mid (ratio, value 0.25)
cust.oem (ratio, value 0.22)
cust.distributor (ratio, value 0.13)
serviceableFactor (ratio, value 0.55)
obtainableFactor (ratio, value 0.01)

HYPOTHESIS:
{
 "id": "frankfurt-grid-queue-de-slip",
 "title": "A single metro's 24–36-month grid queue gates ~37% of the CE TAM's Year-1 flow",
 "narrative": "Germany is 0.50 of the base and Frankfurt hosts ~75% of German IT capacity — so roughly 37% of the venture's TAM routes through one metro's grid interconnection queue, where waits now run 24–36 months and vacancy is 2.2%. The model treats DE as a diversified half of the market; in the first-12-months window it is really a bet that Frankfurt's 542 MW under construction energizes on schedule. If 2026 energizations slip right — grid queue, plus EnEfG efficiency/heat-reuse compliance adding commissioning friction — the fit-outs that would generate the venture's largest tranche of Year-1 PDU purchase orders move to 2027–28 while the 9% trend stays intact on paper.",
 "category": "exogenous",
 "targetNodes": [
  "geo.DE",
  "obtainableFactor"
 ],
 "mechanism": [
  {
   "step": "trigger",
   "text": "Frankfurt grid connections face 24–36-month waits while 542 MW sits under construction and 383 MW in planning — energization slots, not demand, set commissioning dates",
   "refs": [
    "digest:geo-DE#c15",
    "digest:geo-DE#c8",
    "digest:geo-DE#c14"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "propagation",
   "text": "Frankfurt is ~75% of German capacity and DE is 0.50 of the CE base, so a queue slip in one metro defers the largest single block of near-term fit-out demand; PDU POs land at fit-out, so deferred energization means deferred orders regardless of the 2030 CAGR",
   "refs": [
    "digest:geo-DE#c1",
    "ledger:geo.DE"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "model-impact",
   "text": "DE's effective share of the in-window demand flow falls toward the installed-load low bound (0.42) and the Year-1 obtainable pace drops with it — the funnel's biggest geography becomes 2028 revenue",
   "refs": [
    "ledger:geo.DE",
    "ledger:obtainableFactor"
   ]
  }
 ],
 "whyMissable": "The ledger carries country-level shares that look diversified across seven markets; there is no metro dimension, so the fact that half the funnel physically routes through one substation queue is structurally invisible. The DE band (0.42–0.53) reads as scope uncertainty, not as a timing gate.",
 "expectedObviousness": "medium",
 "falsifier": "TenneT/regional grid operator commitments (or JLL Frankfurt prints) showing Frankfurt live IT load stepping up by >100 MW within the venture's Year-1, i.e. the 542 MW under construction energizing on schedule.",
 "likelihood": {
  "value": 0.4,
  "rationale": "Queue lengths and near-zero vacancy are documented; offsetting, projects already under construction typically hold secured grid allocations, so slippage hits the planning cohort harder than the UC cohort.",
  "basis": "evidence"
 },
 "perturbation": [
  {
   "nodeId": "geo.DE",
   "op": "set",
   "value": 0.42,
   "note": "DE share of contestable in-window demand falls to the installed-load low bound as Frankfurt additions slip"
  },
  {
   "nodeId": "obtainableFactor",
   "op": "scale",
   "value": 0.6,
   "note": "Year-1 pace compressed as the largest geography's fit-outs defer"
  }
 ],
 "indicators": [
  {
   "signal": "Frankfurt live IT-load and under-construction prints",
   "where": "JLL German data center market updates and Mordor Frankfurt series",
   "threshold": "<+50 MW live-load growth across two consecutive half-year prints",
   "updates": "increases"
  },
  {
   "signal": "Grid-connection queue length and energization commitments for Rhein-Main data-center applicants",
   "where": "TenneT/Amprion connection disclosures and Hesse permitting dockets; operator capex-call commentary on Frankfurt energization dates",
   "threshold": "Stated waits lengthening beyond 36 months or named projects re-guiding commissioning by ≥2 quarters",
   "updates": "increases"
  }
 ],
 "mitigations": [
  {
   "action": "Commission an energization-date audit of the Frankfurt 542 MW under-construction pipeline (which projects hold firm grid allocations for 2026)",
   "type": "information",
   "voiNodeId": "geo.DE",
   "note": "Separates timing risk from level risk in the DE share"
  },
  {
   "action": "Rebalance the Year-1 territory plan toward Berlin (219 MW in planning) and Poland/CEE, where additions are less grid-queue-bound",
   "type": "strategic",
   "voiNodeId": null,
   "note": null
  },
  {
   "action": "Launch a retrofit/replacement SKU program for operating Frankfurt halls whose demand does not depend on new grid connections",
   "type": "operational",
   "voiNodeId": null,
   "note": null
  }
 ],
 "lens": "demand-discontinuity",
 "riskId": "risk.demand-discontinuity.frankfurt-grid-queue-de-slip"
}

VALIDATION ERROR:
[
  {
    "code": "invalid_value",
    "values": [
      "error",
      "risk"
    ],
    "path": [
      0,
      "resolution"
    ],
    "message": "Invalid option: expected one of \"error\"|\"risk\""
  }
]

Emit the corrected hypothesis JSON (same shape).

The model treats DE as a diversified half of the market; in the first-12-months window it is really a bet that Frankfurt's 542 MW under construction energizes on schedule. operator capex-call commentary on Frankfurt energization dates","threshold":"Stated waits lengthening beyond 36 months or named projects re-guiding commissioning by ≥2 quarters","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Commission an energization-date audit of the Frankfurt 542 MW under-construction pipeline (which projects hold firm grid allocations for 2026)","type":"information","voiNodeId":"geo.DE","note":"Separates timing risk from level risk in the DE share"},{"action":"Rebalance the Year-1 territory plan toward Berlin (219 MW in planning) and Poland/CEE, where additions are less grid-queue-bound","type":"strategic","voiNodeId":null,"note":null},{"action":"Launch a retrofit/replacement SKU program for operating Frankfurt halls whose demand does not depend on new grid connections","type":"operational","voiNodeId":null,"note":null}]}
